procedure_overview: Reverse Shoulder Arthroplasty (arthroscopy_or_endoscopy). Certified. Score 4. Source-backed from v3.
must_know_anatomy: RSA: deltopectoral or superolateral (lateral split) approaches. Relies on deltoid for elevation after reversing ball/socket.
Deltopectoral preferred for most RSA (exposure, extension). Lateral split for some. Subscapularis management varies by implant/design.
surgical_approach_anatomy: RSA: deltopectoral or superolateral (lateral split) approaches. Relies on deltoid for elevation after reversing ball/socket.

structures_at_risk.structure: Axillary nerve (on deep surface of deltoid and inferior to subscapularis)
structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Lies 5-7 cm below acromion on deltoid undersurface and crosses inferior to subscapularis. At risk during inferior glenoid/baseplate work or medial retraction in RSA.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Identify early at inferior subscap border. Keep arm abducted and externally rotated during inferior work. Limit inferior capsular release and use blunt retractors.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Deltoid paralysis (loss of abduction/elevation) and teres minor weakness. Devastating for RSA outcome since deltoid is the primary elevator.
structures_at_risk.approach_context: Deltopectoral or superolateral for RSA (inferior glenoid exposure)

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Enters conjoint tendon deep/medial 5-8 cm below coracoid. Excessive medial retraction in deltopectoral for RSA can injure it.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Limit medial retraction force and duration. Identify before any conjoint release. Know the distance from coracoid.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Weak elbow flexion (biceps/brachialis) and lateral forearm numbness (LABC).

structures_at_risk.why_at_risk: Management (repair vs. not, tenotomy vs. peel) varies by RSA design and affects anterior stability and internal rotation. Poor handling leads to instability or loss of IR.
structures_at_risk.how_to_avoid_injury: Follow implant-specific subscap protocol. Tag and repair if indicated for the chosen RSA prosthesis. Avoid overtensioning.
structures_at_risk.consequence_of_injury: Anterior instability or loss of active internal rotation after RSA.

key_landmarks: Coracoid process: proximal landmark for deltopectoral incision to deltoid insertion.
Acromion: reference for superolateral deltoid split; limit split to 3-5 cm from acromion to protect axillary nerve.
Cephalic vein in deltopectoral groove (retract laterally with deltoid).
Conjoint tendon: musculocutaneous nerve enters deep/medial 5-8 cm below coracoid.
common_mistakes: Inadequate identification of structures at risk leading to iatrogenic injury
Missing key landmarks resulting in malpositioned hardware
Ignoring deforming forces causing loss of reduction
Poor fluoroscopic confirmation of starting point or trajectory
Incomplete release or exposure leading to inadequate decompression or fixation
attending_pimp_questions: Deltopectoral vs lateral approach for RSA; axillary nerve importance post-op.
What are the key structures at risk specific to the main approach or portals for this case?
What is the primary deforming force or pathoanatomy that must be countered during reduction or repair?
Name the main ligaments or intervals that define safe dissection or stable fixation for this procedure.
What fluoroscopic views or landmarks confirm adequate reduction and implant position intraoperatively?
night_before_review_checklist: Review linked modules and source URLs
Confirm any case-specific fluoroscopy or approach details
limitations: Anatomy-focused case prep only; does not replace full surgical technique or attending-specific preferences
Always verify with latest imaging and team briefing

surgical_layers.layer_name: Superficial deltopectoral or superolateral (skin to interval)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: For deltopectoral (preferred for most RSA): incision from coracoid to deltoid insertion. Cephalic vein in groove (retract laterally with deltoid). For superolateral: split deltoid 3-5 cm from acromion (limit to protect axillary).
structures_at_risk: Axillary nerve (deep deltoid 5-7 cm below acromion; limit split)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: Deltopectoral gives extensile exposure and is standard for RSA. Superolateral is shorter but risks more axillary nerve issues if split too far.

surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Identify conjoint tendon (musculocutaneous n 5-8 cm below coracoid - limit medial retraction). Subscapularis tenotomy or peel (management varies for RSA stability). Anterior capsule and glenoid exposure. Deltoid is the primary elevator post-RSA (no rotator cuff reliance).
key_structures: Glenoid and humerus (for baseplate and stem)
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: RSA specific: subscap handling affects stability; deltoid is now the key muscle. Axillary protection remains critical (inferior work).

surgical_layers.layer_name: Glenoid and humeral preparation (RSA specific exposure)
surgical_layers.what_user_should_know: Glenoid exposure for baseplate (inferior tilt, version). Humeral preparation (stem version, height for deltoid tension). Trial reduction and stability testing with the reversed ball-and-socket. No subscap repair in some RSA designs.
surgical_layers.surgical_relevance: The RSA-specific working layer. Deltoid tensioning is more important than anatomic reconstruction. Axillary nerve at highest risk during inferior glenoid exposure.

validation_warnings: Reverse shoulder arthroplasty payload is deltopectoral-primary for RSA. Anatomic TSA content is in the separate total_shoulder_arthroplasty payload. RSA relies on deltoid tensioning rather than anatomic reconstruction. Axillary nerve at highest risk during inferior glenoid work. Source material from 12000/12061; further RSA-specific pearls recommended.
approach_specific_notes: Strictly for reverse shoulder arthroplasty (RSA), primarily via deltopectoral (preferred for exposure and extensibility). Deltoid is the primary elevator (no rotator cuff reliance). Axillary nerve protection (5-7 cm deep deltoid) and musculocutaneous (5-8 cm on conjoint) remain critical. Subscapularis management is prosthesis-specific. No anatomic TSA or cuff repair content mixed. Lateral split is alternative but has higher axillary risk if extended.
